notes: Questions chosen to exercise the skill's mandate: trade-off analysis, cross-source weighting, consensus-vs-contested claim handling, failure-mode enumeration. Each has documented disagreement in the literature so the skill's reasoning standards (distinguish claim types, flag contradictions, confidence levels, steelman alternatives) should matter.

evals.name: tdd-empirical-evidence
evals.prompt: I'm a tech lead deciding whether to mandate test-driven development (TDD) across my team. What does the empirical research actually say about TDD's effect on defect rate, productivity, and code quality in industrial settings? Be specific about what is and isn't supported by the evidence, where studies contradict each other, and what confounds the findings.
evals.reference_answer: Summary of the actual evidence base: The TDD literature is genuinely contested, not clearly positive. Key points a strong answer must convey: (1) There are ~8 systematic literature reviews (SLRs) on TDD (Kollanus 2011, Sfetsos & Stamelos, Turhan et al., Causevic et al., Makinen & Munch, Munir et al., Bissi et al., Abushama et al.); overlap between them is only ~54% on response variables and only ~3% of primary studies appear in all 8 SLRs. This means different reviews examine different evidence bases and reach different conclusions. (2) Defect/external-quality: some studies show 40-50% defect density reduction (Nagappan/Microsoft 4-team industrial study, Williams/IBM), but Muller & Hagner 2002 found TDD was worse initially in the implementation phase and only slightly better after acceptance testing. Rafique & Misic 2013 meta-analysis found a small positive effect on quality (effect size ~0.3) with high heterogeneity. (3) Productivity: evidence is mixed-to-negative. Most studies show productivity decreases 15-35% with TDD in the short term; long-term productivity effects are not reliably measured. (4) Major confounds that weaken most studies: process conformance (whether participants actually followed TDD) is often not measured; test granularity and test-first vs test-frequently conflated; experience level of developers swamps the TDD effect; student-vs-industrial populations give different results; often TDD is compared against ITLD (iterative test-last) vs waterfall, changing the baseline. Ghafari 2020 ("Why Research on TDD is Inconclusive") argues the field has structural methodological problems making strong claims unsupportable. (5) Consensus vs contested: strong consensus that TDD increases test coverage and number of tests written. Mixed evidence on external quality (small positive effect, high variance). Mixed-to-negative on short-term productivity. No reliable evidence on long-term maintainability. (6) A tech-lead recommendation should acknowledge: mandating TDD on the basis of empirical evidence is not supportable; the evidence supports it as a reasonable practice with modest quality benefits and short-term productivity costs, not as a proven silver bullet. Better framed as a team-culture / design-feedback tool than a defect-reduction intervention. Risks of mandating: Hawthorne effect wearing off, bad tests written to satisfy the ritual, developers working around the rule. Steelman of the opposing view: the Coplien/DHH criticism that TDD produces over-mocked brittle tests and a design bias toward testable-not-good code. Confidence levels: strong (TDD increases tests written), moderate (small positive quality effect), weak (productivity effects), insufficient (long-term maintainability). Further investigation: measure baseline defect rate and test coverage, run a 3-6 month opt-in pilot, distinguish TDD adherence from test-first-sometimes.

evals.name: microservices-vs-modular-monolith
evals.prompt: My company has ~30 engineers working on a single product. Leadership wants to migrate our modular monolith to microservices because 'we're at the scale where that's what you do.' I'm skeptical. What does the current thinking say about when microservices actually pay off vs when they're premature, specifically for a team of our size? What are the concrete failure modes I should warn leadership about?
evals.reference_answer: The recent (2024-2026) industry consensus has shifted noticeably away from microservices-by-default toward modular monoliths as the correct default for most teams. Key points a strong answer must convey: (1) Team size heuristics from practitioner consensus: <20 engineers = modular monolith clearly correct; 20-50 engineers = modular monolith usually correct, microservices only if specific domains scale differently or need independent release cadence; 50+ engineers = microservices start to pay off for domain autonomy; 100+ = microservices clearly correct for most orgs. At 30 engineers the skeptic is right: the default is monolith. (2) The migration-back trend: Amazon Prime Video (2023) moved a component from microservices to monolith for 90% cost reduction; several case studies in 2024-2026 document similar moves. The industry narrative has swung from 'microservices good' (2015-2020) to 'modular monolith good, microservices only when you need them' (2023-2026). (3) The actual driver of microservices is NOT team size per se, it's (a) independent deployment cadence per domain, (b) different scaling profiles per domain, (c) different language/runtime needs per domain, (d) regulatory/security isolation. Absent these, microservices add cost without benefit. (4) Concrete failure modes to warn leadership about: distributed monolith (services coupled at runtime but deployed separately, worst of both worlds); transaction boundaries crossing services (needing sagas, eventual consistency, or distributed transactions); operational overhead (30-40% infrastructure cost increase is typical; K8s expertise required; observability/tracing essential); debugging across service boundaries (no stack trace, need distributed tracing); data consistency issues (CAP trade-offs become every team's problem); deployment coordination (service A needs service B v2, now you need contract tests or versioning); on-call burden multiplied by N services; Conway's Law inverted (you now need to reorg to match service boundaries); resume-driven architecture (the team wants microservices because it's interesting, not because it helps). (5) Evidence quality: the specific claims (30-40% cost overhead, 5x complexity) come from practitioner blog posts and vendor reports (DataDog, Honeycomb, Camunda surveys), not peer-reviewed research. Treat as experiential wisdom, not empirical fact. Academic research on microservices is sparse; Fowler, Newman (Building Microservices 2nd ed), and Shahin/Babar/Zhu systematic reviews are the usual citations. (6) Recommended direction: push back on the migration; propose alternative framings — what specific pain is leadership trying to solve (deploy conflicts? scaling one component? team boundaries?)? If the pain is real, extract 1-2 services that genuinely need independence (the 'strangler' approach, or 'microservices for the edges, monolith for the core'). If the pain is vague, decline and invest in the monolith's modularity (bounded contexts, module boundaries, internal APIs, async event bus within one deployable). (7) Steelman the microservices case: if your monolith's build/test cycle is >20min, if one team's deploys consistently break another's, if you have drastically different scaling profiles (e.g., ML inference vs CRUD), the case gets stronger. (8) Confidence: strong consensus (at 30 engineers, modular monolith is the default); moderate confidence (specific cost/complexity multipliers); contested (the 'when exactly to split' threshold). Further investigation: DORA metrics on current monolith, deploy conflict frequency, which domains actually have different scaling needs.

evals.name: rust-vs-go-network-services
evals.prompt: We're building a new high-traffic API gateway / network proxy and the team is debating Rust vs Go. Both have strong production stories. Cut through the hype: what do the actual benchmarks and production case studies show about their trade-offs for this kind of workload? Where does each genuinely win and where is the claimed advantage overstated?
evals.reference_answer: A strong answer must distinguish claims the evidence supports from claims that are overstated marketing. Key points: (1) Performance — what the numbers actually say: On average throughput for simple HTTP handling, Go and Rust are comparable (within 20-30%), with Rust frameworks like Actix-web ~1.5x faster than Go fiber in some benchmarks. BUT on tail latency (p99, p99.9), Rust's advantage widens significantly because Go's GC causes stop-the-world pauses. Discord's 2020 migration case study showed 10x memory reduction and 5x tail latency reduction for a read-heavy service — this is the single most-cited data point. Cloudflare's Pingora (Rust replacement for nginx) handles ~1T requests/day; similar story at Figma, 1Password. (2) Where Rust's advantage is real and where it's overstated: Real: tail latency under GC pressure, memory footprint (2-4x lower typical), CPU efficiency under load, no GC tuning. Overstated: average throughput (often within 20% of Go), developer productivity at steady state (eventually comparable after 6-12mo), ecosystem breadth for networking (Go's net/http and gRPC-Go are more mature and battle-tested than Rust equivalents as of 2024-2026, though Tokio/Hyper/Tonic have closed the gap). (3) Where Go genuinely wins: developer velocity (hire-and-productive in 1-2 weeks vs 2-3 months for Rust), build times (Go sub-second incremental, Rust 10-60s even with sccache/mold), ecosystem maturity for cloud-native (k8s, operators, protobuf, most CNCF projects in Go), error handling ergonomics for typical service code, goroutines simplicity vs Rust's async (Send/Sync/Pin/lifetime soup in async Rust is real cost), hiring pool. (4) Concrete trade-off framing for an API gateway specifically: if your p99 latency target is sub-ms and you're CPU-bound, Rust's no-GC guarantee is a genuine architectural advantage. If your p99 target is 5-50ms and you're I/O-bound on upstream services, GC pauses are below your measurement noise and Go is the better choice. (5) Failure modes by language: Rust — async complexity (Send/Sync across await), compile-time cost on CI, cargo dependency depth, smaller hiring pool, risk of premature abstraction via traits/generics. Go — GC tail latency spikes under load, error handling verbosity, nil pointer panics, interface pollution, goroutine leaks, no sum types means weaker domain modeling. (6) Polyglot as a real option: the 2026 trend is not Rust-vs-Go but both-in-their-lane — Go for orchestration/control plane/high-level services, Rust for inference/proxies/data-plane hot paths. (7) Source quality: the specific 5x / 10x figures come from Discord's blog post and other practitioner posts; the general-benchmark numbers from TechEmpower are heavily contested (microbenchmarks don't reflect production); the strongest evidence is case studies (Discord, Cloudflare Pingora, Figma, Dropbox's earlier Python-to-Go and later Rust pieces). Treat specific multipliers as illustrative, not universal. (8) Recommendation framing: for an API gateway, if sub-ms tail latency matters → Rust (Pingora is the reference implementation). If <50ms is acceptable → Go is fine and faster to ship. Don't pick Rust for resume/cool points; don't pick Go because you've always used Go. Confidence: strong consensus on tail latency; strong consensus on developer velocity trade-off; moderate on ecosystem comparison (narrowing fast); contested on 'crossover point' where Rust is worth the cost. Further investigation: measure your actual p99 target, benchmark a prototype of the hot path in both, estimate hiring/onboarding cost for your region.

evals.name: creatine-cognition-healthy-adults
evals.prompt: Creatine monohydrate is heavily marketed as a cognitive enhancer now, not just a muscle-building supplement. As a healthy adult in my 30s, is there actually good evidence it improves cognition, or is this the next nootropic hype cycle? Be specific about which cognitive domains are supported, which aren't, and how strong the evidence actually is.
evals.reference_answer: A strong answer must separate what the meta-analytic literature actually supports from marketing-driven overreach. Key points: (1) Evidence landscape: multiple recent systematic reviews and meta-analyses exist (Prokopidis et al. 2023 in Nutrition Reviews on memory; Xu et al. 2024 in Frontiers in Nutrition on cognition broadly; Sandkuhler et al. 2023 in Neuroscience & Biobehavioral Reviews; Forbes et al. 2022 on older adults). Results are genuinely mixed. (2) What is supported (modest evidence): Memory — small positive effect in healthy adults, more reliable in older adults (60+); effect sizes typically g=0.2-0.3 (small). Cognition under stress/sleep-deprivation — moderate positive effects in sleep-deprived subjects. Processing speed / reaction time — small positive effect in some meta-analyses, not others. Vegetarians and vegans — larger effects because baseline creatine intake is lower; 2003 Rae et al. study is the canonical citation. (3) What is NOT well supported: Executive function — no reliable effect in healthy young adults. General/overall cognitive function — no reliable effect. Long-term cognitive enhancement in healthy rested young adults at baseline — weak and contested. (4) Critical methodological caveats that the marketing obscures: EFSA's 2024 scientific opinion on creatine and cognition found that Xu et al.'s meta-analysis pooled non-independent cognitive test results from the same subjects, inflating the effective sample size; EFSA concluded 'no conclusions could be drawn' from that data. This is a major red flag specifically against the most-cited 2024 positive meta-analysis. Publication bias is likely; many small positive trials and few large well-powered ones. Wash-out periods often inadequate. Doses vary 3-20g/day. Loading-phase vs maintenance-phase effects confounded. (5) Population differences matter: older adults respond more (brain creatine stores decline with age); vegetarians respond more (lower baseline); sleep-deprived respond more; healthy rested omnivore young adults respond least. A 30-year-old omnivore at adequate sleep is in the lowest-effect population. (6) Safety and risks: creatine monohydrate is one of the most-studied supplements; no meaningful adverse effects at 3-5g/day in healthy adults (some water retention, minor GI at loading doses). This is NOT where the concerns are. (7) Claim types to distinguish: 'Creatine improves muscle performance in resistance training' = strong empirical consensus (hundreds of RCTs). 'Creatine improves cognition in sleep-deprived or vegetarian subjects' = moderate evidence. 'Creatine is a cognitive enhancer for healthy rested adults' = weak evidence, and the recent marketing wave overstates what is known. (8) Recommendation framing for a healthy 30-year-old: if you already take creatine for training, there may be a small cognitive bonus; standalone for cognitive effects the expected benefit is small and possibly zero. Not hype in the sense of being dangerous or useless (it is a well-supported supplement for other reasons), but yes hype in the sense that 'cognitive enhancer' is getting oversold relative to the evidence. Confidence: strong (muscle/strength benefit, safety); moderate (cognition in specific vulnerable populations); weak (general cognitive enhancement in healthy young adults); contested (magnitude of memory effect, given EFSA methodological critique). (9) Steelman of the pro-cognition view: brain creatine is measurable via MRS and increases with supplementation; there's a biological mechanism (ATP buffering in neurons), and the effect in stressed populations is consistent. Steelman of the skeptical view: publication bias, non-independent pooling, small effect sizes, inconsistent across meta-analyses. Further investigation: check your baseline diet (vegetarian?), sleep quality, and stress level — these predict whether you'll see any effect. A 3-month self-trial at 3-5g/day with a cognitive task battery is cheap and low-risk but methodologically weak.
